output.final_deliverable:
I cannot complete the population comparison because no web-evidence (merged results) was provided in your request. The merged-results array is empty, so there are no source pages or extracted facts to use. Required missing items

1) Missing evidence: the merged results array is empty. I need at least three source URLs or search+visit results (or permission to perform them) to extract population figures and dates. Without those, I must not invent or assume any population values.

What I will produce once evidence is provided or I am allowed to search

- For each of three authoritative sources (one government, one news outlet, one research institution) I will extract and list explicitly the following labeled facts:
  1. Source name (e.g., U.S. Census Bureau)
  2. Population figure (exact value as shown, e.g., "873,965")
  3. Date of the data (exact date or period shown, e.g., "2023 estimate" or "April 1, 2020")
  4. Direct citation URL (full URL)

- I will present each source as a numbered item, for example:
  1. Government: Source name — Population: <exact number> — Date: <exact date/period> — URL: <full URL>
  2. News outlet: Source name — Population: <exact number> — Date: <exact date/period> — URL: <full URL>
  3. Research institution: Source name — Population: <exact number> — Date: <exact date/period> — URL: <full URL>

- If you request URLs or a chain, I will list them in order and explicitly show any links between pages (e.g., "URL[1] contains link to URL[2]").

How I will identify discrepancies and decide which figure is most likely accurate

- I will compare the exact population numbers and the dates/periods they reference.
- I will identify differences and possible reasons, including:
  - different reference dates (e.g., 2020 Census vs 2023 estimate),
  - different estimation methods (official census counts vs annual estimates vs journalist rounding),
  - whether a figure is a city population vs a metro-area population.
- I will indicate which figure is most likely accurate based on these criteria (preference order typically: most recent official government estimate from an authoritative agency, followed by reputable research institutions using clear methodology, and then reputable news outlets that cite the original source). I will explain the reason and cite the exact supporting URLs.

What I need from you (pick one)

- Option A — Provide the three source URLs you want compared (one government, one news outlet, one research institution). I will extract the facts and return the requested comparison and reasoning.
- Option B — Give me permission to perform web searches and visits now. I will fetch up-to-date authoritative pages (examples I would target: U.S. Census Bureau population estimates for San Francisco, City/County of San Francisco official population estimate, a major news outlet report such as San Francisco Chronicle or The New York Times, and a research institution such as the Public Policy Institute of California or Pew Research Center), extract exact figures/dates, and produce the comparison with full URLs.

If you choose Option A, please paste the URLs. If you choose Option B, reply "Search now" and I will fetch and extract the three items and return them with full citations.

Why I cannot proceed right now

- The merged results are empty, so there are no provided pages from which to extract the required exact numbers, dates, and URLs. Per your instruction and the extraction rules, I must use only provided evidence; nothing was provided.

If you want an immediate prepared template of the final output format (with placeholders), I can return that as well.
output.action_summary: No population figures were extracted because the merged-results array is empty. I need either (A) three source URLs you want used, or (B) permission to perform web searches now. Once I have source pages, I will extract for each: exact population number, exact date/period, and full citation URL; list them clearly; identify discrepancies with reasons; and state which figure is most likely accurate and why, citing URLs.

7f7f0e24-6edf-43b6-9333-5e5c5f740e36.title: Search — Government sources for San Francisco population (city / federal)

details.query: San Francisco population site:sf.gov OR site:census.gov "San Francisco" population estimate 2022 2023 2024
details.intent?: Locate the most authoritative government-reported population figures for San Francisco (e.g., City & County of San Francisco pages, U.S. Census Bureau: decennial census and latest population estimates / ACS).
target_facts?: Date or year that figure refers to (e.g., '2023 estimate', '2020 census')
Exact citation URL (page that states the figure)
Source organization (e.g., U.S. Census Bureau, City & County of San Francisco)
verification_steps?: Prefer pages with explicit datestamp and methodology (e.g., 'population estimates' page or ACS table).
If multiple government pages found, record the most recent official estimate and note if it's an ACS estimate vs. decennial census.
Capture the exact text stating the figure to avoid transcription errors.
details.why_this_node?: A government source is the primary authoritative source for population figures; this search will produce candidate URLs for an official figure to be visited and extracted exactly.
details.goal: Search — Government sources for San Francisco population (city / federal)
details.original_goal: Search — Government sources for San Francisco population (city / federal)
details.parent_goal: Find the current population of San Francisco as reported by three different authoritative sources (government, news outlet, and research institution). For each source, provide: the population figure, the date of the data, and a direct citation URL. Then identify any discrepancies between the sources and explain which figure is most likely accurate and why.

678fc311-5f92-41ff-b851-b02c54a310c7.title: Visit — Government source page to extract population, date, and citation

details.intent?: Open the top government URL returned by the preceding search and extract the exact population number, the date of the data (estimate year or census year), and the exact citation URL.
target_facts?: population_value: string/number (exact text as on page)
methodology_note: string (if page indicates ACS, estimate, or census)
extraction_plan?: Read the page top-to-bottom (first 6000 chars) and locate explicit population statements.
If the page links to a PDF or table with the figure, follow that link and extract the figure and date from the linked document (capture the linked URL too).
Record whether the number is an estimate, a census count, or an ACS-derived figure.
details.why_this_node?: Visiting the government page is necessary to get the authoritative figure, datestamp, and a direct citation URL — all required by the mandate.
details.goal: Visit — Government source page to extract population, date, and citation
details.original_goal: Visit — Government source page to extract population, date, and citation
details.parent_goal: Find the current population of San Francisco as reported by three different authoritative sources (government, news outlet, and research institution). For each source, provide: the population figure, the date of the data, and a direct citation URL. Then identify any discrepancies between the sources and explain which figure is most likely accurate and why.

1fabf5e8-3a1a-487c-b372-b32bfb22ddf9.title: Search — News outlet reporting on San Francisco population (recent article)

details.query: San Francisco population reported by news article 2023 2024 "San Francisco population" site:nytimes.com OR site:sfgate.com OR site:sfgate.com OR site:sfchronicle.com OR site:latimes.com
details.intent?: Find a reputable news outlet article that cites a San Francisco population figure (with date) — pick a widely recognized outlet (e.g., SF Chronicle, LA Times, NYT) for comparison.
target_facts?: Population figure cited by the article
Date of the data the article references
Whether the article cites an underlying source (e.g., Census Bureau, city statement)
verification_steps?: Prefer articles that explicitly state the source and date of their population figure.
Capture the article publication date and any quoted source for cross-checking.
details.why_this_node?: A news outlet may report a different figure or interpretation (estimates vs. counts); finding a reputable article provides the 'news outlet' data point required by the mandate.

ba3040de-e46d-4ef8-a943-bebba4d89b58.title: Visit — News outlet article to extract population, date, and citation

details.intent?: Open the news article URL found by the previous search and extract the population number cited, the date of the data referenced, the article publication date, and the exact URL.
target_facts?: population_date: string (the date/year the article says the data is from)
extraction_plan?: Read the article’s relevant paragraphs where population is mentioned and capture the exact phrasing and numeric figure.
Note whether the article is quoting a government source or presenting its own estimate.
If the article links to a source (e.g., Census page), capture that link as well.
details.why_this_node?: Extracting the news outlet's stated figure and the article's sourcing is required to compare the news-reported figure to government and research institution sources.

cfdd4117-3f17-49a2-ad7d-01da102f65f4.title: Search — Research institution / think tank / university population report for San Francisco

details.query: San Francisco population site:prb.org OR site:population.ucla.edu OR site:urban.org OR site:berkeley.edu "San Francisco population" 2022 2023 2024
details.intent?: Find a figure from a research institution or academic center (e.g., Population Reference Bureau, university population research center, urban institute) that reports a San Francisco population value and methodology/date.
verification_steps?: Prefer institutional pages or brief reports that clearly state the data source and date.
If the institution reports its own calculations, capture the method and underlying data source.
details.why_this_node?: A research institution may use different methods or datasets; collecting this figure helps identify discrepancies and methodological differences.
